name: Badarayana
dates_notes: Date range spans four centuries; identity with Vyāsa is traditional, not academic; only the Brahma-Sūtra survives, and even its layers may be multi-author.

lineage_polemical.note: Brahma-Sūtra 2.1.3 (etena yogaḥ pratyuktaḥ) extends the previous adhikaraṇa's Sāṃkhya-refutation to Yoga, on the grounds that Yoga shares the Sāṃkhya pradhāna-kāraṇa-vāda.

Brahma-Sūtra 2.2.37 (the pati-pāśupata-pakṣa sūtra: 'patyur asāmañjasyāt') refutes the Pāśupata position: Śiva-as-nimitta-kāraṇa-only fails to give a coherent account of the Lord's relation to the world. The position the sūtra refutes is the Lakulīśa-Pāśupata pati-paśu-pāśa triad.

Brahma-Sūtra 2.2.37 refutes the Pāśupata position; Kauṇḍinya's Pañcārtha-Bhāṣya is the principal interpretive vehicle that makes the Lakulīśa-Sūtra's compressed sūtras concrete enough for the Brahma-Sūtra to engage.

Brahma-Sūtra 2.2.42–45 (the utpatty-asambhavādhikaraṇa) refutes the Pāñcarātra vyūha-doctrine on the ground that the production of one vyūha-form from another is incoherent (Saṅkarṣaṇa as jīva produced from Vāsudeva would be either created soul or eternal Lord, and either reading is problematic).

Brahma-Sūtra 2.2.11–17 refutes the Vaiśeṣika paramāṇu-kāraṇa-vāda — the doctrine that atoms are the world-cause — through the structural impossibility of unconscious atoms initiating the first conjunction at sarga.

Brahma-Sūtra 2.2.33–36 refutes the Jaina position broadly conceived: anekānta is rejected as collapsing into self-contradiction, and the doctrine of the soul's pradeśa-extension to body-size is rejected as incoherent. Umāsvāti is the foundational figure of the position refuted.

Brahma-Sūtra 3.3.53–54 engages the Lokāyata position that the self is identical with the body and refutes it; the engagement is the canonical Vedāntic acknowledgement of the position as a darśana to be answered.

Brahma-Sūtra 1.1.4 (tat tu samanvayāt) is read by every later Vedāntic bhāṣyakāra as Bādarāyaṇa's response to the Mīmāṃsā prioritization of the karma-kāṇḍa: śruti's tātparya converges on brahman, not merely on enjoined ritual action. The Brahma-Sūtra also cites Jaimini by name on several disputed adhikaraṇas (e.g., 1.2.31, 3.4.2, 4.4.5).

Brahma-Sūtra 2.2.18–32 mounts the canonical Vedāntic refutation of the Buddhist schools. The Mādhyamaka-aimed strand within that adhikaraṇa-sequence (especially the sarva-vaināśika-pakṣa-nirākaraṇa) targets the doctrine of universal momentary destruction and, by extension, the śūnyatā-thesis read as a denial of any abiding ground.
Brahma-Sūtra 2.2.28–32 mounts the canonical Vedāntic refutation of the Yogācāra vijñāna-vāda; the developed Vasubandhu-Triṃśikā / Viṃśatikā position stands within the targeted vijñāna-only doctrines.

Brahma-Sūtra 2.2.18–20 mounts the canonical Vedāntic refutation of the Sarvāstivāda-Vaibhāṣika position. The sūtras target the foundational sarvam asti thesis and the kṣaṇika-vāda (momentariness) on which it rests; Vasubandhu's Abhidharma-Kośa-Bhāṣya is the principal systematic exposition of those theses available to the Vedāntic critic, with the Sautrāntika-leaning glosses of the bhāṣya providing internal evidence for the difficulties the orthodox position faces.

core_thesis: What discipline does a Vedāntic inquirer enter, and on what authority? Bādarāyaṇa answers in his first two *sūtras*. *Athāto brahma-jijñāsā* (1.1.1) — "now, therefore, the inquiry into *brahman*" — opens the discipline, and *janmādy asya yataḥ* (1.1.2) defines its object as "that from which the origin and the rest of this [world]" proceeds. *Brahman* is thus introduced under one descriptive function: the *jagat-kāraṇa*, world-cause. The work then unfolds across four *adhyāyas* whose headings name four argumentative moves: *samanvaya* ([*Brahma-Sūtra* 1.1.4](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/1.1.4) *tat tu samanvayāt*: harmonization of *śruti* texts as teaching *brahman*), *avirodha* ([*Brahma-Sūtra* 2.1–2.3](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/2.1-2.3): rebuttal of rival cosmologies), *sādhana* ([*Brahma-Sūtra* 3.3–3.4](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/3.3-3.4): the means — meditative *upāsanā*, Vedic *karma*'s relation to knowledge), *phala* ([*Brahma-Sūtra* 4.3–4.4](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/4.3-4.4): the fruit — *mokṣa* and the trajectory of the *jīva* after death). On the *jīva*–*brahman* relation Bādarāyaṇa cites three earlier ācāryas — Auḍulomi ([*Brahma-Sūtra* 1.4.21, 4.4.6](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/1.4.21, 4.4.6)), Āśmarathya ([*Brahma-Sūtra* 1.2.29, 1.4.20](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/1.2.29, 1.4.20)), Kāśakṛtsna ([*Brahma-Sūtra* 1.4.22](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/1.4.22)) — without adjudicating their dispute. The compressed style of the *sūtra* genre forces every later reader to supply a *bhāṣya*; this is why a single text becomes the shared ground of Advaita, Viśiṣṭādvaita, Tattva-vāda (Dvaita), and Bhedābheda. Whether Bādarāyaṇa's underdetermination is deliberate or merely the inheritance of an oral *sūtra*-corpus is itself an open question.

engaged_works.title: Brahma-Sūtra
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored as a compiled text; the work is multi-layered and likely incorporates earlier sūtra-material.

ascription_evidence.description: Modern academic consensus accepts the Brahma-Sūtra as a unified compiled text traditionally ascribed to Bādarāyaṇa, with internal-style evidence of multi-layered sūtra-material.

engaged_works.summary: An aphoristic treatise: 555 *sūtras* in four *adhyāyas*, each subdivided into four *pādas*. *Adhyāya* 1 establishes *samanvaya* — that the disputed *śruti* passages all converge on *brahman* as their *tātparya*. The decisive sūtra here is 1.1.4, *tat tu samanvayāt*. *Adhyāya* 2 turns to *avirodha*: rival cosmologies are refuted in turn — Sāṅkhya *pradhāna* (2.1–2.2), Vaiśeṣika atomism, Buddhist schools (Sautrāntika, Vijñānavāda, Mādhyamika), Jaina *anekānta*, Pāśupata, Pāñcarātra. *Adhyāya* 3 treats *sādhana*: *upāsanā*, the *jīva*'s transmigration, the relation of *karma* to *vidyā*. *Adhyāya* 4 treats *phala*: *devayāna*, *kramamukti*, the condition of the *mukta-puruṣa*. The work cites by name the ācāryas Auḍulomi, Āśmarathya, Kāśakṛtsna, Bādari, Jaimini, and Kārṣṇājini; this is direct evidence that what survives is the consolidation of a prior oral *sūtra*-tradition. The text is unintelligible without a commentary, and this is precisely why it can serve as common ground for incompatible schools. Securely a compiled work; the question of single versus multi-author redaction remains open. Standard editions: Thibaut (SBE 34, 38), Deussen, and the Ānandāśrama series of the major *bhāṣyas*.
